content.text: The state of the atmosphere over an area at any point of time. It refers to short-term, localized atmospheric conditions that can change rapidly even within a day.

The sum total of weather conditions and variations over a large area for a long period of time (more than thirty years). It represents the generalised, long-term pattern of atmospheric conditions in a region.

Weather is temporary and changeable, while climate is permanent and consistent. For example, a single rainy day represents weather, but the pattern of rainfall over thirty years represents climate. Weather conditions fluctuate frequently even within a day, but climate patterns emerge only over extended periods.

content.title: Elements of Climate and Weather
content.text: Both weather and climate are described using the same elements: temperature, atmospheric pressure, wind, humidity, and precipitation. These five elements together define the atmospheric conditions of any area.

On the basis of generalised monthly atmospheric conditions, the year is divided into seasons such as winter, summer, and rainy seasons. These seasons represent predictable patterns in climate that repeat annually.

Seasonal variations are particularly noticeable in interior parts of the country, while coastal areas experience more uniform conditions throughout the year due to the moderating influence of the sea.

notes.section_title: India's Climatic Classification and Regional Variations

content.text: Derived from the Arabic word 'mausim' meaning season. Monsoon refers to the seasonal reversal in wind direction during a year, characterised by strong winds that reverse direction between seasons.

India's climate is described as 'monsoon' type. In Asia, this type of climate is found mainly in the south and the southeast. Despite overall unity in the general climate pattern, there are perceptible regional variations in climatic conditions within the country.

content.title: Why Monsoon Climate?
content.text: The monsoon type of climate is characterised by a distinct seasonal pattern with significant changes in weather from one season to another. These changes are primarily caused by the seasonal reversal of wind systems, which brings moisture-laden winds to the subcontinent.

India exhibits wide regional variations in temperature. In summer, the mercury occasionally touches 50°C in parts of the Rajasthan desert, while in the same season, Pahalgam in Jammu and Kashmir may experience around 20°C. This difference of 30°C demonstrates the extreme variation across the country.

In winter, temperatures are equally varied. Drass in Jammu and Kashmir may experience temperatures as low as minus 45°C, while Thiruvananthapuram on the southern coast may have a temperature of around 22°C.

content.title: Diurnal Range of Temperature
content.text: There is a wide difference between day and night temperatures in certain places. In the Thar Desert, the day temperature may rise to 50°C and drop to near 15°C the same night — a range of 35°C. In contrast, there is hardly any difference in day and night temperatures in the Andaman and Nicobar Islands or in Kerala, where the moderating influence of the sea is strong.

Precipitation in India varies significantly in form, type, amount, and seasonal distribution. While precipitation in the upper parts of the Himalayas is mostly in the form of snowfall, it rains over the rest of the country.

The annual precipitation varies from over 400 cm in Meghalaya (one of the wettest places on Earth) to less than 10 cm in Ladakh and western Rajasthan. This range of nearly 40 times demonstrates the extreme variation in rainfall across India.

Most parts of the country receive rainfall from June to September. However, some parts like the Tamil Nadu coast receive a large portion of their rain during October and November, showing seasonal variation in rainfall arrival.

In general, coastal areas experience less contrasts in temperature conditions due to the moderating influence of the sea. The sea acts as a heat reservoir, absorbing heat in summer and releasing it in winter, keeping coastal areas warmer in winter and cooler in summer.

Seasonal contrasts are more pronounced in the interior of the country, where the absence of the moderating influence of the sea results in very hot summers and very cold winters. This condition is known as continentality.

There is a general decrease in rainfall from east to west in the Northern Plains. Eastern regions like West Bengal receive more rainfall, while western regions like Rajasthan receive considerably less.

There are six major controls of the climate of any place: latitude, altitude, pressure and wind system, distance from the sea (continentality), ocean currents, and relief features. These controls interact together to determine the climate of a region.

Due to the curvature of the earth, the amount of solar energy received varies according to latitude. As a result, air temperature generally decreases from the equator towards the poles. Regions closer to the equator receive more direct and intense solar radiation, resulting in higher temperatures.

In India's context, the Tropic of Cancer passes through the middle of the country from the Rann of Kuchchh in the west to Mizoram in the east. Almost half of the country lying south of the Tropic of Cancer belongs to the tropical area, while the remaining area north of the Tropic lies in the sub-tropics. Therefore, India's climate has characteristics of both tropical and subtropical climates.

As one goes from the surface of the earth to higher altitudes, the atmosphere becomes less dense and temperature decreases. The hills are therefore cooler during summers compared to plains at the same latitude.

India has mountains to the north with an average height of about 6,000 metres, and also a vast coastal area where the maximum elevation is about 30 metres. The Himalayas prevent the cold winds from Central Asia from entering the subcontinent.

content.title: Himalayan Influence
content.text: It is because of the Himalayas that the Indian subcontinent experiences comparatively milder winters as compared to Central Asia. Without this mountain barrier, the subcontinent would experience extremely cold winters similar to interior Asian regions at the same latitude.

An apparent force caused by the earth's rotation. The Coriolis force is responsible for deflecting winds towards the right in the northern hemisphere and towards the left in the southern hemisphere. This is also known as Ferrel's Law.

The climate and associated weather conditions in India are governed by three atmospheric conditions: pressure and surface winds, upper air circulation, and western cyclonic disturbances and tropical cyclones.

India lies in the region of north easterly winds. These winds originate from the subtropical high-pressure belt of the northern hemisphere. They blow southwards, get deflected to the right due to the Coriolis force, and move towards the equatorial low-pressure area. Generally, these winds carry little moisture as they originate and blow over land, bringing little or no rain.

content.title: The Paradox of Indian Climate
content.text: Given that north easterly winds carry little moisture and should make India an arid land, the question arises: why is India not arid? The answer lies in the seasonal reversal of wind systems during summer, when south-west monsoon winds bring abundant moisture from the ocean.

The pressure and wind conditions over India are unique. During winter, there is a high-pressure area north of the Himalayas. Cold dry winds blow from this region to the low-pressure areas over the oceans to the south.

In summer, a low-pressure area develops over interior Asia as well as over northwestern India. This causes a complete reversal of the direction of winds during summer. Air moves from the high-pressure area over the southern Indian Ocean in a south-easterly direction, crosses the equator, and turns right (due to Coriolis force) towards the low-pressure areas over the Indian subcontinent. These are known as the South-West Monsoon winds.

The South-West Monsoon winds blow over the warm oceans, gather abundant moisture, and bring widespread rainfall over the mainland of India. This seasonal reversal of winds is the defining characteristic of the monsoon climate.

topics.topic_name: Distance from the Sea and Ocean Currents

content.text: The sea exerts a moderating influence on climate. As the distance from the sea increases, its moderating influence decreases and the people experience extreme weather conditions. This condition is known as continentality — characterised by very hot summers and very cold winters.

Ocean currents along with onshore winds affect the climate of coastal areas. A coastal area with warm or cold currents flowing past it will be warmed or cooled if the winds are onshore. Warm currents increase temperature while cold currents decrease temperature in coastal regions.

Relief plays a major role in determining the climate of a place. High mountains act as barriers for cold or hot winds, preventing their passage to leeward regions. Mountains may also cause precipitation if they are high enough and lie in the path of rain-bearing winds.

content.title: Rain Shadow Effect
content.text: The leeward side of mountains remains relatively dry because the windward side receives most of the rainfall as moist air is forced to rise. By the time air descends on the leeward side, it has lost most of its moisture. This is why regions east of the Western Ghats receive less rainfall than the western coast.

The monsoon type of climate is characterised by a distinct seasonal pattern. The weather conditions greatly change from one season to the other. These changes are particularly noticeable in the interior parts of the country, while coastal areas do not experience much variation in temperature, though there is variation in rainfall pattern.

Four main seasons can be identified in India — the cold weather season (winter), the hot weather season (summer), the advancing monsoon (rainy season), and the retreating monsoon (transition season). Some regional variations in seasonal patterns exist.

topics.topic_name: The Cold Weather Season (Winter: November - February)

content.text: The cold weather season begins from mid-November in northern India and stays till February. December and January are the coldest months in the northern part of India.

The temperature decreases from south to north. The average temperature of Chennai on the eastern coast is between 24°–25° Celsius, while in the northern plains, it ranges between 10°C and 15° Celsius. Days are warm and nights are cold. Frost is common in the north and the higher slopes of the Himalayas experience snowfall.

During this season, the northeast trade winds prevail over the country. They blow from land to sea and hence, for most parts of the country, it is a dry season. However, some amount of rainfall occurs on the Tamil Nadu coast from these winds as here they blow from sea to land.

In the northern part of the country, a feeble high-pressure region develops, with light winds moving outwards. Influenced by relief, these winds blow through the Ganga valley from the west and northwest. The weather is normally marked by clear sky, low temperatures, low humidity, and feeble, variable winds.

content.title: Winter Cyclonic Disturbances
content.text: A characteristic feature of the cold weather season over the northern plains is the inflow of cyclonic disturbances from the west and northwest. These low-pressure systems originate over the Mediterranean Sea and western Asia and move into India along with the westerly flow. They cause much-needed winter rains over the plains and snowfall in the mountains. Although the total amount of winter rainfall, locally known as 'mahawat', is small, it is of immense importance for the cultivation of 'rabi' crops (winter crops).
The peninsular region does not have a well-defined cold season due to the moderating influence of the sea. There is hardly any noticeable seasonal change in temperature pattern during winters.

topics.topic_name: The Hot Weather Season (Summer: March - May)

content.text: Due to the apparent northward movement of the sun, the global heat belt shifts northwards. As such, from March to May, it is the hot weather season in India.

The influence of the shifting heat belt can be seen clearly from temperature recordings. In March, the highest temperature is about 38° Celsius on the Deccan plateau. In April, temperatures in Gujarat and Madhya Pradesh are around 42° Celsius. In May, temperature of 45° Celsius is common in the northwestern parts of the country. In peninsular India, temperatures remain lower due to the moderating influence of the oceans.

The summer months experience rising temperature and falling air pressure in the northern part of the country. Towards the end of May, an elongated low-pressure area develops in the region extending from the Thar Desert in the northwest to Patna and Chotanagpur plateau in the east and southeast.

content.title: The Loo
content.text: A striking feature of the hot weather season is the 'loo' — strong, gusty, hot, dry winds blowing during the day over north and northwestern India. Sometimes they continue until late in the evening. Direct exposure to these winds may prove to be fatal. These winds are caused by the extreme heating of the land surface and the resulting pressure gradient.

Dust storms are very common during May in northern India. These storms bring temporary relief as they lower the temperature and may bring light rain and cool breeze. This is also the season for localised thunderstorms, associated with violent winds, torrential downpours, and often accompanied by hail. In West Bengal, these storms are known as 'Kaal Baisakhi'.

Towards the close of the summer season, pre-monsoon showers are common, especially in Kerala and Karnataka. They help in the early ripening of mangoes and are often referred to as 'mango showers'. These showers signal the approaching arrival of the monsoon.

topics.topic_name: The Advancing Monsoon (Rainy Season: June - September)

content.text: By early June, the low-pressure condition over the northern plains intensifies. It attracts the trade winds of the southern hemisphere. These south-east trade winds originate over the warm subtropical areas of the southern oceans. They cross the equator and blow in a south-westerly direction, entering the Indian peninsula as the south-west monsoon.

As these winds blow over warm oceans, they bring abundant moisture to the subcontinent. These winds are strong and blow at an average velocity of 30 km per hour. With the exception of the extreme northwest, the monsoon winds cover the country in about a month.

The inflow of the south-west monsoon into India brings about a total change in the weather. Early in the season, the windward side of the Western Ghats receives very heavy rainfall, more than 250 cm. The Deccan Plateau and parts of Madhya Pradesh also receive some amount of rain despite lying in the rain shadow area.

The maximum rainfall of this season is received in the north-eastern part of the country. Mawsynram in the southern ranges of the Khasi Hills receives the highest average rainfall in the world, approximately 11,873 mm annually. Rainfall in the Ganga valley decreases from the east to the west. Rajasthan and parts of Gujarat get scanty rainfall.

content.title: Breaks in Monsoon
content.text: Another phenomenon associated with the monsoon is its tendency to have 'breaks' in rainfall. Thus, it has wet and dry spells — the monsoon rains take place only for a few days at a time, interspersed with rainless intervals. These breaks in monsoon are related to the movement of the monsoon trough. The trough and its axis keep moving northward or southward, determining the spatial distribution of rainfall.

When the axis of the monsoon trough lies over the plains, rainfall is good in these parts. Whenever the axis shifts closer to the Himalayas, there are longer dry spells in the plains and widespread rain occurs in the mountainous catchment areas of the Himalayan rivers. These heavy rains bring devastating floods, causing damage to life and property in the plains.

The frequency and intensity of tropical depressions determine the amount and duration of monsoon rains. These depressions form at the head of the Bay of Bengal and cross over to the mainland, following the axis of the monsoon trough of low pressure. The monsoon is known for its uncertainties — the alternation of dry and wet spells vary in intensity, frequency, and duration. While it causes heavy floods in one part, it may be responsible for droughts in another.

topics.topic_name: The Retreating Monsoon (Transition Season: October - November)

content.text: During October-November, with the apparent movement of the sun towards the south, the monsoon trough or the low-pressure trough over the northern plains becomes weaker. This is gradually replaced by a high-pressure system. The south-west monsoon winds weaken and start withdrawing gradually. By the beginning of October, the monsoon withdraws from the Northern Plains.

The months of October-November form a period of transition from the hot rainy season to dry winter conditions. The retreat of the monsoon is marked by clear skies and rise in temperature. While day temperatures are high, nights are cool and pleasant. The land is still moist.

Owing to the conditions of high temperature and humidity, the weather becomes rather oppressive during the day. This is commonly known as 'October heat'. In the second half of October, the mercury begins to fall rapidly in northern India.

content.title: Cyclonic Activity
content.text: The low-pressure conditions over north-western India get transferred to the Bay of Bengal by early November. This shift is associated with the occurrence of cyclonic depressions, which originate over the Andaman Sea. These cyclones generally cross the eastern coasts of India and cause heavy and widespread rain. These tropical cyclones are often very destructive. The thickly populated deltas of the Godavari, the Krishna, and the Kaveri are frequently struck by cyclones, causing great damage to life and property.

Parts of the western coast and northeastern India receive over about 400 cm of rainfall annually. These areas are influenced by the south-west monsoon winds which bring moisture from the Arabian Sea and the Bay of Bengal.

However, rainfall is less than 60 cm in western Rajasthan and adjoining parts of Gujarat, Haryana, and Punjab. These regions lie in the rain shadow of the Western Ghats or are too far from the influence of the monsoon.

Rainfall is equally low in the interior of the Deccan plateau and east of the Sahyadris (Western Ghats). A third area of low precipitation is around Leh in Jammu and Kashmir, which is in the rain shadow of the Himalayas. The rest of the country receives moderate rainfall.
Snowfall is restricted to the Himalayan region and areas of high altitude due to low temperatures.

Owing to the nature of monsoons, the annual rainfall is highly variable from year to year. Variability is high in the regions of low rainfall, such as parts of Rajasthan, Gujarat, and the leeward side of the Western Ghats.

content.title: Drought and Flood Vulnerability
content.text: As such, while areas of high rainfall are liable to be affected by floods, areas of low rainfall are drought-prone. The monsoon's irregular arrival, its variability in intensity, and its uneven distribution often disturb the farming schedule of millions of farmers all over the country.

notes.section_title: The Monsoon as a Unifying Force

content.text: The Himalayas protect the subcontinent from extremely cold winds from Central Asia, enabling northern India to have uniformly higher temperatures compared to other areas on the same latitudes. Similarly, the Peninsular plateau, under the influence of the sea from three sides, has moderate temperatures.

Despite such moderating influences and great variations in the temperature conditions, the unifying influence of the monsoon on the Indian subcontinent is quite perceptible. The seasonal alteration of the wind systems and the associated weather conditions provide a rhythmic cycle of seasons.

Even the uncertainties of rain and uneven distribution are very much typical of the monsoons and are accepted as part of the Indian climate. The Indian landscape, its animal and plant life, its entire agricultural calendar, and the life of the people, including their festivities, revolve around this phenomenon.

content.title: Monsoon as a Cultural and Agricultural Bond
content.text: Year after year, people of India from north to south and from east to west eagerly await the arrival of the monsoon. These monsoon winds bind the whole country by providing water to set the agricultural activities in motion. The river valleys which carry this water also unite as a single river valley unit. The monsoon is not just a climatic phenomenon but a unifying force that connects India's geography, agriculture, culture, and people.

notes.section_title: Climate and Human Life in India

topics.topic_name: Climate-Related Adaptations in Housing and Lifestyle

content.text: The variations in climate have given rise to variety in the lives of people in terms of the food they eat, the clothes they wear, and also the kind of houses they live in.

content.title: Examples of Climate-Based Adaptations
items: Houses in Rajasthan have thick walls and flat roofs to protect from extreme heat and the intense sun, with thick walls providing insulation.
Houses in the Tarai region and in Goa and Mangalore have sloping roofs to allow rain water to drain quickly and prevent waterlogging in high rainfall areas.
Houses in Assam are built on stilts (elevated structures) to protect from frequent flooding caused by heavy monsoon rainfall and river overflow.

glossary.definition: Derived from Arabic 'mausim' meaning season. Refers to the seasonal reversal in wind direction during a year, characterised by strong winds that blow in opposite directions in winter and summer, bringing distinct rainfall patterns.
The sum total of weather conditions and variations over a large area for a long period of time (more than thirty years), representing the generalised pattern of atmospheric conditions in a region.
The state of the atmosphere over an area at any point of time. Short-term, localized atmospheric conditions that can change rapidly within a day or even hours.
Angular distance north or south from the equator, measured in degrees. Determines the amount of solar energy received and affects temperature distribution globally and locally.
Height above sea level. As altitude increases, the atmosphere becomes less dense and temperature decreases, affecting climate conditions.

The condition experienced when distance from the sea increases, resulting in extreme weather conditions — very hot summers and very cold winters — due to the absence of the moderating influence of the sea.

An apparent force caused by the earth's rotation, responsible for deflecting winds towards the right in the northern hemisphere and towards the left in the southern hemisphere. Also known as Ferrel's Law.
The relatively dry region on the leeward side of a mountain where moisture-bearing winds have lost most of their moisture after crossing the windward side, resulting in low precipitation.

The moisture-laden winds that blow from the southern Indian Ocean in a south-westerly direction (turning right due to Coriolis force) during summer (June-September), bringing abundant rainfall to India.
Dry winds originating from the subtropical high-pressure belt of the northern hemisphere, blowing southwards from land to sea during winter, generally carrying little moisture.

An imaginary line passing through the middle of India from the Rann of Kuchchh in the west to Mizoram in the east at approximately 23.5° north latitude, dividing tropical and subtropical climate zones.
The difference between the maximum and minimum temperatures in a 24-hour period. Large in deserts (like Thar: 35°C) but minimal in coastal areas due to ocean moderation.
Strong, gusty, hot, dry winds blowing during the day over north and northwestern India during the hot weather season (March-May), often dangerous due to extreme heat.
An elongated low-pressure area that forms over the Indian subcontinent during the monsoon season, whose position determines the spatial distribution of rainfall across different regions.
Winter crops sown in October-November and harvested in March-April, dependent on winter rainfall (mahawat) for their cultivation in northern India.

A severe rotating storm originating over warm ocean waters (especially the Andaman Sea), bringing heavy rainfall and destructive winds when crossing Indian coasts, particularly affecting eastern deltas.
Local term for winter rainfall in northern India, brought by cyclonic disturbances from the Mediterranean region, crucial for rabi crop cultivation despite small total amounts.

summary_points: India's climate is classified as monsoon type, characterised by seasonal reversal of wind direction, bringing distinct winter (dry) and summer (wet) patterns.
The Tropic of Cancer divides India into tropical (south) and subtropical (north) zones, giving India characteristics of both climatic types.
Temperature varies from 50°C in Rajasthan deserts to -45°C in Drass (Kashmir); diurnal range is extreme in deserts (35°C) but minimal in coastal areas due to sea moderation.
Annual rainfall varies from over 400 cm in Meghalaya to less than 10 cm in Ladakh and western Rajasthan, with most rain falling June-September except Tamil Nadu coast (October-November).
Six climatic controls determine India's climate: latitude, altitude, pressure and wind systems, distance from sea (continentality), ocean currents, and relief features.
The Himalayas act as a barrier preventing cold Central Asian winds, enabling comparatively milder winters in India; they also cause rain shadow effects on leeward sides.
Four main seasons characterise India: Cold Weather (Nov-Feb), Hot Weather (Mar-May), Advancing Monsoon (Jun-Sep), and Retreating Monsoon (Oct-Nov), each with distinct weather patterns.
South-West Monsoon winds blow from June onwards, bringing moisture from oceans and causing widespread rainfall; their arrival and intensity determine agricultural success and water availability.
Monsoon breaks (wet and dry spells) create alternating dry and wet periods; monsoon movement determines regional rainfall distribution, causing floods in some areas and droughts in others.

Despite regional variations, the monsoon acts as a unifying force binding India's geography, agriculture, culture, and people across all regions, determining the agricultural calendar and lifestyle patterns.
Climate-based human adaptations are evident in housing: thick walls in Rajasthan, sloping roofs in high-rainfall areas, and stilts in flood-prone regions, reflecting regional climatic challenges.
